questions.q: What is the primary purpose of a robots.txt file?
options: To block all web traffic to your site
To instruct search engine crawlers which pages to crawl and which to exclude
To improve website security by blocking hackers
To increase page load speed
questions.explanation: robots.txt directs crawler behavior and prevents crawling of sensitive or duplicate content. It doesn't block traffic (that's firewalls) or improve security; it's a crawl budget optimization tool, not a security mechanism.

questions.q: What does a canonical tag do in SEO?
options: It removes duplicate content from your site
It tells search engines which version of a page is the preferred original
It automatically redirects traffic between pages
It prevents all other sites from linking to your pages
questions.explanation: Canonical tags signal the authoritative version of duplicate content without removing pages or creating redirects. They consolidate ranking signals but don't physically remove pages; other sites can still link freely despite canonicals.

questions.q: Why is page speed important for SEO rankings?
options: It has no impact on rankings
Google uses Core Web Vitals as a ranking factor, and fast pages improve user experience
Only mobile speed matters, desktop speed is irrelevant
Page speed only affects ad revenue, not organic search
questions.explanation: Page speed is a confirmed ranking signal for both desktop and mobile; Core Web Vitals assess user experience. Slow pages hurt both rankings and conversions; speed affects organic search visibility, not just ads.

questions.q: What is the relationship between crawl budget and site structure?
options: Crawl budget doesn't exist; Google crawls everything equally
Poor site structure wastes crawl budget on unimportant pages instead of priority content
Crawl budget only applies to large enterprise sites, not small sites
More internal links automatically increase crawl budget
questions.explanation: All sites have finite crawl budgets; efficient architecture directs crawlers to valuable content. Poor structure (deep nesting, dead links) wastes budget on low-value pages; crawl budget applies to all sites; internal links should be strategic, not excessive.

questions.q: What is the purpose of an XML sitemap in technical SEO?
options: To replace the need for internal links
To inform search engines about your site structure and priority pages
To guarantee all pages will be indexed
To improve website security
questions.explanation: Sitemaps guide crawlers to important pages and signal update frequency, helping indexing but not guaranteeing it. Sitemaps supplement (not replace) internal navigation; indexing still depends on crawlability and relevance; sitemaps have no security function.

questions.q: How do 301 redirects versus 302 redirects differ in their SEO impact?
options: They have identical SEO impact
301 (permanent) passes authority while 302 (temporary) does not preserve link equity reliably
302 redirects are faster than 301 redirects
Only 302 redirects are indexed by search engines
questions.explanation: 301s signal permanent moves and consolidate authority; 302s indicate temporary changes, causing search engines to potentially crawl both versions and dilute signals. For permanent URL changes, 301 is always correct; 302 wastes ranking potential.

questions.q: What does the Core Web Vitals assessment include?
options: Keyword density and heading count
Largest Contentful Paint, First Input Delay, and Cumulative Layout Shift
Domain age and backlink count
Page title length and meta description accuracy
questions.explanation: Core Web Vitals measure user experience: LCP (load time), FID (interactivity), and CLS (visual stability). They don't evaluate keywords, age, or backlinks; these specific metrics directly impact mobile rankings and user satisfaction.

questions.q: How does HTTPS/SSL certification affect SEO rankings?
options: HTTPS has no impact on SEO
Google favors HTTPS sites and treats it as a weak ranking signal
HTTPS is required for all sites or they'll be de-indexed
HTTPS only benefits e-commerce sites, not content sites
questions.explanation: HTTPS is a confirmed ranking signal; secure sites receive a modest boost over non-secure versions. It's not required for indexing but is strongly recommended; security matters across all site types for user trust and search visibility.

questions.q: What is the purpose of mobile-first indexing in Google's crawling strategy?
options: To penalize desktop-only websites
Google crawls and ranks pages based primarily on mobile versions due to most traffic being mobile
It only applies to sites built with mobile frameworks
Mobile-first indexing eliminates the need for desktop optimization
questions.explanation: Google predominantly crawls and indexes mobile versions since most users browse mobile. This doesn't penalize desktop or eliminate desktop optimization; it means mobile UX and mobile site structure are now the primary ranking factors.

questions.q: How do structured data and schema markup improve technical SEO?
options: They guarantee higher rankings automatically
They help search engines understand content context and enable rich snippets in results
They are only useful for e-commerce product pages
Schema markup has been deprecated and no longer works
questions.explanation: Schema markup provides context (JSON-LD, Microdata) enabling rich snippets, knowledge panels, and better crawling. It doesn't guarantee rankings but improves CTR through enhanced SERP appearance; it applies to content, events, articles, and more—not just products.
